title: Partial sums & series
description: Determine whether series converge or diverge based on their partial sums.
topic_path: Defining convergent and divergent infinite series

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=-\frac{2n}{(n+1)(n+2)}$.

$S=$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}{{S}_{n}}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(-\frac{2n}{(n+1)(n+2)}\right)=0$

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=\frac{2}{n}-\frac{3}{n}-4$.

$S=$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left( \frac{2}{n}-\frac{3}{n}-4 \right)=-4$

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=\frac{3}{2}+\frac{2n}{4n+7}$.

$S=$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac{3}{2}+\frac{2n}{4n+7}\right)=\frac{3}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=2$

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=\frac{n+1}{n^2+1}$.

$S=$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The value of the series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}{{S}_{n}}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n+1}{n^2+1}=0$

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=6-\frac{3}{{{n}^{2}}}$.

$S=$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.
$\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left( 6-\frac{3}{{{n}^{2}}} \right)=6$

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=2-\frac{4n}{{{n}^{3}+6}}$.

$S=$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The value of the series is the limit, as $n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left( 2-\frac{4n}{{{n}^{3}+6}} \right)=2$

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=\frac{n-1}{n+1}$.

$S=$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}{{S}_{n}}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n-1}{n+1}=1\,$

assessment_items.question_content:
**If the $~n\text{th}~$ partial sum of the series $~\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ is given by $\displaystyle~{{S}_{n}}=\frac{2n^3}{(n+1)(n+2)}\,$, which of the following is true in regard to $~S~?$**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\qquad\displaystyle S=\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{S}_{n}}\,$.

$\qquad\displaystyle\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{S}_{n}}=\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\left(\frac{2n^3}{(n+1)(n+2)}\right)=\infty$

Hence, $S$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $\displaystyle ~S=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ be an infinite series such that the $~n\text{th}~$ partial sum is given by $~\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=\frac{2}{n}+4n\,$.

**Which of the following is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

Hence,$\qquad\displaystyle S=\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{S}_{n}}\,$.

$\qquad\displaystyle\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\left( \frac{2}{n}+4n \right)=\infty$

Hence, $S$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=6n-\frac{3}{{{n}^{2}}}$.

**Determine $S$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.

$\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left( 6n-\frac{3}{{{n}^{2}}} \right)=\infty$

Hence, $S$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=~\frac{n^2+1}{n+1}$.

**Determine $S$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The value of the series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.

$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}{{S}_{n}}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n^2+1}{n+1}=\infty$

Hence, $S$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
The $n\text{th}$ partial sum of the series $S$ is given by

$\displaystyle{{S}_{n}}=\frac{n^2-1}{n+1}$

**Determine $S$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The value of an infinite series is the limit, as $~n\to\infty\,$, of the sequence of partial sums.

That is, $\displaystyle S=\lim_{n\to\infty}S_n$.

$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}{{S}_{n}}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n^2-1}{n+1}=\infty$

So $S$ diverges.
